candidate_sources.title: Alder - Sooty Mold - Cooperative Extension: Insect Pests, Ticks and Plant Diseases - University of Maine Cooperative Extension
candidate_sources.snippet: University of Maine Cooperative Extension factsheet dedicated to Sooty Mold on Alder, with images and information on the dark superficial fungal growth caused by sooty mold fungi growing on honeydew secreted by sap-sucking insects such as aphids and scale insects (e.g., Scorias spongiosa associated with woolly alder aphid).

candidate_sources.title: Sooty Molds | NC State Extension Publications
candidate_sources.snippet: NC State Extension publication on sooty molds: dark, superficial mycelium that gives leaves and branches a charcoal-gray sooty appearance. Sooty molds are not pathogenic — they grow on honeydew excreted by aphids, scales, mealybugs, psyllids, and whiteflies. Common genera include Capnodium, Fumago, and Scorias (Scorias spongiosa is associated with aphid and scale pests of alder, pine, beech and other trees).

candidate_sources.title: Sooty Mold / Home and Landscape / UC Statewide IPM Program (UC IPM)
candidate_sources.snippet: UC IPM Pest Notes on Sooty Mold: a fungal disease that grows on honeydew produced by sucking insects (aphids, scales, mealybugs, psyllids, whiteflies). Fungi most commonly causing sooty mold on landscape plants are in the genera Capnodium, Fumago, and Scorias. Management focuses on controlling the honeydew-producing insects and ants that tend them; horticultural oils and washing with water can help remove existing sooty mold.
